figures.caption: Table 2: Per-term feature encoding for the 13 Pauli-string terms of the L = 4 XXZ Hamiltonian in Table 1 (∆ = 0.25, g = 1.8). The log |c| column uses file-stored coefficients under the spin-12 convention (Sα = 1 2σ α), so two-body terms carry a factor of 1 4 and single-site terms a factor of 1 2 relative to the bare Pauli operators. The Pauli column is a six-way one-hot (X, Y , Z, XX, Y Y , ZZ); body indicates whether the term is single-qubit (0) or two-body (1); dist is the inter-qubit distance; m1,m2,m3,m4 are the color-group indices from Table 1. The global context shared across all terms is shown below the rule.

Figure 4: Error landscape: fidelity gap f∗ − fmodel versus transverse field g for each Trotter regime, averaged over five seeds on test sizes L ∈ {16, 17, 18, 19, 20}. Color indicates anisotropy ∆, and the dashed line marks the poor-prediction threshold (gap = 0.01). Under first-order Trotter (top row), the poor-prediction rate remains low at 0.7% for r = 3 and 1.8% for r = 5. Under second-order Trotter (bottom row), failures are more frequent, with poor-prediction rates of 6.0% for r = 3 and 6.0% for r = 5. The largest gaps concentrate at g ≳ 2.0, where oracle fidelity is already low and differences between candidate orderings become more pronounced.

Figure 1: Fidelity of random and structured Trotter orderings across system sizes L = 5, 10, 16 for the Néel initial state, first-order product formula, and r = 5 Trotter steps. For each Hamiltonian instance, sorted by best random-ordering fidelity, we show 30 random orderings (amber dots), their best, worst, and mean fidelities (green, red, and orange), and all 24 structured candidates (blue squares). At L = 5, random and structured orderings are often comparable. By L = 10, a clear gap emerges, and at L = 16 the structured candidates consistently occupy the high-fidelity region while random orderings are concentrated lower. This trend shows that structured orderings become increasingly advantageous as system size grows.

Figure 3: Selected-ordering fidelity versus oracle fidelity for the learned model and the strongest non-learned baseline, MajRegime, across held-out test instances (L ∈ {16, 17, 18, 19, 20}). Points are colored by method and shaped by Trotter regime (p, r), and the dashed line marks oracle-level performance y = x. The learned selector remains more tightly concentrated near the oracle line overall, while MajRegime shows substantially larger deviations, especially in the lower-fidelity regime.

Figure 2: Overview of the coloring-aware Trotter ordering pipeline, divided into two stages. Dataset generation (left): For a given 1D XXZ Hamiltonian (L,∆, g), we construct the commutation graph C(H) from Pauli terms and apply four coloring methods (xyz groups, greedy, gurobi, handcrafted), producing C = 24 candidate orderings (4×3! = 24). For each candidate ordering, we compute the fidelity F (σ) = |⟨ψexact|ψTrotter(σ)⟩|2 between the exact and Trotter-evolved states. These fidelities are used during training only: the oracle fidelity, defined as the best fidelity among the 24 structured candidates, provides the supervision label. Model learning (right): Per-term local features and global context are fed into a transformer encoder with four self-attention layers, four heads, and attention pooling. A classification head maps the pooled representation to a softmax distribution over C = 24 classes; the argmax selects σ∗ at inference without evaluating candidate fidelities.

Figure 6: Sample efficiency: mean test fidelity gap versus the number of Hamiltonians per system size used in training. Colored lines show held-out test sizes L ∈ {16, 17, 18, 19, 20}, and the dashed line marks the average MajRegime reference. By nham = 30, all plotted test sizes fall below the MajRegime reference and stabilize thereafter, indicating that 30 Hamiltonians per system size are sufficient for near-oracle generalization in this setting.

Table 3: Per-regime breakdown of the three evaluation metrics, averaged over five seeds and all held-out test sizes L ∈ {16, . . . , 20}. Gap: test fidelity gap f∗ − fmodel, averaged over test instances (lower ↓ is better). Norm: normalized gain (fmodel − f rand)/(f∗ − f rand) on instances where f∗ > f rand (higher ↑ is better). Poor : fraction of instances with gap > 0.01 (lower ↓ is better). All model metrics are computed without evaluating candidate fidelities at inference time. The model performs near-oracle under first-order Trotter across both step counts. Errors concentrate in the second-order regime (poor-prediction rate up to 6.0%); even so, the model achieves normalized gain close to 1.0 and substantially improves over MajRegime in overall mean gap, with the largest improvement in the hardest second-order r=3 regime.

Figure 5: Generalization sweep. Mean fidelity gap versus system size L for each validation size Lmax ∈ {5, . . . , 15}, where training uses L ∈ {3, . . . , Lmax − 1} and evaluation is performed on larger sizes up to L = 20 (one line per Lmax, mean over three seeds). The shaded region marks the final held-out test range L ∈ {16, . . . , 20}, and the dashed horizontal line marks the MajRegime reference. Lines for Lmax ≤ 8 remain near 10−2 on larger systems, indicating limited generalization. Generalization emerges at Lmax = 9, and lines for Lmax ≥ 13 compress toward 10−3 with little growth across system size, confirming clean extrapolation to larger systems.
